vsl.title: The Partnership Decision: What Every Dental Practice Owner Needs to Know Before Signing with a DSO

sections.name: Opening Hook
sections.script:
[OPEN ON: Close-up of a dentist's hands -- not operating, but typing on a laptop at a desk cluttered with insurance forms, payroll documents, and a cold cup of coffee. It is late. The office is empty.]

NARRATOR (V.O.):
"Seven out of ten.

That is the number that should terrify every dental practice owner in America.

According to the Dentist Advisors podcast, seven out of ten dentists who sell to a DSO end up frustrated, annoyed, or outright regretful.

One dentist told them: 'I wish I would have never done this.' He had $10 million in cash.

So the question is not whether to explore partnership. The question is: how do you end up in the other 30%?"

[MUSIC: Low, tension-building ambient score begins]
sections.bRollDirection: Close-up of dentist's hands doing paperwork late at night. Empty office. Cold coffee. The visual should evoke exhaustion and isolation -- the administrative burden of solo ownership.
sections.notes: Pattern interrupt with the 7-out-of-10 statistic. This is the most powerful data point in the entire SGA messaging arsenal. Do not soften it. Let it land.

sections.name: Problem Agitation
sections.script:
NARRATOR (V.O.):
"You did not go to dental school to become a CEO.

You went because you loved the work. The precision. The craft. The moment a patient sees their smile for the first time. That is what pulled you in.

But somewhere along the way, the business ate the dentistry.

You are the HR department. The compliance officer. The marketing director. The accounts receivable specialist. The IT department when the server crashes on a Monday.

You are doing payroll in QuickBooks at midnight. You are Googling HIPAA regulations at 6 AM. You are interviewing hygienists for the third time this year because the last two left for a corporate office that offered better benefits.

And the worst part? Nobody told you this was the deal. Nobody in dental school said: 'By the way, you will spend 40% of your career doing work that has nothing to do with teeth.'

The ADA's own data tells the story: dentist burnout is at historic levels. The staffing crisis is not improving -- 76% of practices report hygienist recruiting as 'extremely challenging.' And practice valuations? They are at cyclical highs right now, but interest rates and market saturation could compress those multiples in the next 2-3 years.

You built something real. A practice with your name on it. Patients who trust you. A team that depends on you.

But the model is breaking. And you can feel it.

Every Sunday night, when the anxiety hits about the week ahead -- that is not clinical anxiety. That is the weight of running a business that was never designed to be run by one person."

[B-ROLL SEQUENCE: Montage of practice owner life -- early morning arrival, reviewing insurance claims, frustrated phone call, empty waiting room during lunch while owner does admin, driving home in the dark]

[MUSIC: Tension builds]
sections.bRollDirection: Documentary-style montage showing the administrative burden: spreadsheets, insurance forms, empty office late at night, owner eating lunch at desk while doing paperwork, driving home after dark.
sections.notes: This section must feel personal and specific -- not generic 'business is hard' content. Use exact scenarios that practice owners recognize from their own lives.

sections.name: False Solutions
sections.script:
NARRATOR (V.O.):
"So you start looking for answers.

Maybe you hire a practice management consultant. They charge $5,000 a month, give you a binder of KPIs, and leave. Nothing changes because the problem is not your metrics. The problem is that you are one person doing seven jobs.

Maybe you try to hire an associate. But the recruiting pipeline is dry. The good ones want mentorship, equity, and benefits you cannot afford to offer as a solo owner. You post on DentalPost for six months and get three applications -- two unqualified, one who ghosts after the interview.

Maybe you look at DSOs. You take a meeting. The business development rep is polished. The pitch deck is beautiful. They throw around terms like 'clinical autonomy' and 'brand preservation' and 'partnership.'

But then you talk to a dentist who actually sold to one. And you hear the other side.

'They changed my name within 18 months.'
'I have a regional manager who has never held a handpiece telling me how to run my schedule.'
'Crown days. They call them crown days.'
'I wish I would have never done this.'

Seven out of ten.

The false solution is not partnership itself. Partnership can work. The false solution is choosing the wrong model -- the wrong structure, the wrong sponsor, the wrong culture -- and discovering it 18 months too late.

So what separates the 30% who are happy from the 70% who are not?"

[B-ROLL: Practice owner at computer researching, scrolling through DSO websites, reading Dentaltown forums, skeptical expression]

[MUSIC: Transition from tension to curiosity]
sections.bRollDirection: Owner researching at computer. Close-ups of DSO marketing materials and pitch decks. Cut to Dentaltown forum posts showing frustration. Skeptical expression on owner's face.
sections.notes: Name the false solutions specifically. The audience needs to see themselves in these failed attempts before they will be open to the real solution.

sections.name: Origin Story
sections.script:
NARRATOR (V.O.):
"Dr. Mitch Ellingson knows this feeling.

For 13 years, he owned practices in Phoenix, Arizona. He was a founding leader at Spear Education -- one of the most respected CE platforms in dentistry. He built Spear Practice Solutions. He had seen the inside of hundreds of dental practices.

And he watched the consolidation wave hit.

He watched colleagues sell to DSOs and come back frustrated, diminished, regretting the decision. He watched corporate operations teams -- people who had never held a handpiece -- dictate clinical protocols to 20-year veterans. He watched the profession he loved start to look like medicine: consolidated, corporatized, and controlled by people who had never treated a patient.

His words: 'I love being a dentist, and I want to protect my profession from becoming like medicine. In medicine, as it consolidated it became less about the doctors and patients, and more about the organization.'

So he decided to build something different.

He partnered with Lamonte Jensen -- a Marine Corps infantry officer turned McKinsey consultant turned dental executive. Jensen had the business architecture. Ellingson had the clinical credibility. Together, they asked a question nobody else in the DSO industry was asking:

What if the business served the dentist -- instead of the other way around?

They founded Gen4 Dental Partners. They grew from 5 practices to 90 in 20 months. They partnered with the Thurston Group -- a private equity firm with 38 years of healthcare platform experience and over $4 billion returned to partners. They merged with MODIS Dental Partners, anchored by Dr. Michael Pikos and the Pikos Institute.

The result is SGA Dental Partners -- a platform built by a dentist, for dentists, with the infrastructure to actually deliver on the promises that every other DSO only puts on a pitch deck."

[B-ROLL: Photos and footage of Ellingson in clinical setting, Jensen at whiteboard, practice settings, Pikos Institute, 4Front Symposium footage]

[MUSIC: Shifts to hopeful, forward-moving]
sections.bRollDirection: Dr. Ellingson in clinical setting -- real, not staged. Jensen at whiteboard planning. B-roll of actual SGA partner practices. Footage from 4Front Symposium showing peer collaboration. Pikos Institute classroom.
sections.notes: Origin story establishes WHY SGA exists -- not what it does. The emotional arc is: frustration with the industry -> decision to build something different -> credibility to actually do it.

sections.name: Three Pillars / Solution
sections.script:
NARRATOR (V.O.):
"SGA is built on three pillars that no other DSO in America can replicate.

PILLAR ONE: CLINICAL AUTONOMY THAT IS STRUCTURAL, NOT ASPIRATIONAL.

Every DSO claims clinical autonomy. SGA builds it into the contract.

The Clinical Independence Clause specifies that treatment plans, materials, lab selection, scheduling, and clinical protocols remain with the partner dentist. The Chief Clinical Officer is Dr. Mitch Ellingson -- a practicing dentist, not a corporate executive.

Dental Partners of Southwest Georgia is still Dental Partners of Southwest Georgia. Periodontal Associates of Memphis is still Periodontal Associates of Memphis. Innovative Dental of Springfield is still Innovative Dental of Springfield. Five years later. Ten years later.

Your name. Your team. Your way of practicing. Those are not marketing promises. They are contractual obligations.

PILLAR TWO: AN EDUCATION PLATFORM THAT DOES NOT EXIST ANYWHERE ELSE.

The Pikos Institute -- founded by Dr. Michael Pikos, one of the most recognized names in implantology worldwide. The 4Front Symposium -- an annual gathering where partner clinicians teach partner clinicians. The Individual Growth Engine -- a 1-on-1 mentorship program that pairs experienced clinicians with partners pursuing new capabilities.

Dr. Matt Cavendish is the proof. 20 years in practice. Good numbers. But in his own words: 'Sometimes it is not as fun as it used to be.' After joining SGA, he was mentored, learned all-on-four, and his revenue grew 16.5% trailing twelve months. Staff retention: 100%.

His word for the experience? Fun. After 20 years. Fun.

No other DSO has an education platform like this. MB2 has zero clinical development infrastructure. Heartland offers generic CE. SALT, Guardian, DCA -- nothing comparable. The Pikos Institute and 4Front Symposium are the moat.

PILLAR THREE: A PE SPONSOR WITH 38 YEARS AND $4 BILLION IN RETURNS.

The Thurston Group. Founded in 1988. Eight dental and healthcare platforms built. Smile Doctors. US Endodontics Partners. US Oral Surgery Management. ARC Health. Over $4 billion returned to partners across the firm's history.

This is not a first-time fund experimenting with your career. This is a firm that has built this exact model eight times and refined it each time.

Every other DSO's PE sponsor is either undisclosed, unproven, or running a 3-to-5-year flip playbook. Thurston's model is patient capital -- compound value over time, not strip and sell.

And SGA will put you on the phone with partners from PRIOR Thurston platforms who have been through a full investment cycle, including an exit. They can tell you exactly what happened. No filter. No agenda."

[B-ROLL: Practice interiors showing preserved branding. Pikos Institute classroom. 4Front Symposium footage. Dr. Cavendish working. Thurston Group building. Charts showing $4B returns.]

[MUSIC: Confident, building momentum]
sections.bRollDirection: Three distinct visual segments matching each pillar. Pillar 1: Practice signage showing preserved names. Pillar 2: Pikos Institute classroom, 4Front event, mentorship pairing. Pillar 3: Professional footage of Thurston Group, historical platform logos, financial performance graphics.
sections.notes: This is the core of the VSL. Each pillar must land with specific proof -- not claims. Names, numbers, and verifiable facts.

sections.name: Social Proof
sections.script:
NARRATOR (V.O.):
"Do not take our word for it. Take theirs."

[CUT TO: Partner testimonial footage]

DR. MITCH ELLINGSON (on camera):
"We tell them we are not a DSO. We are not playing by their rules. We are here to change dentistry. And if you do not embody that and believe that inside of your soul, you should not join Gen4. Because we are not saying it just to attract people. We are saying it because that is who we are."

DR. BRIAN HARRIS (on camera):
"This organization is fully invested in us -- in us loving what we do and being successful in what we do so we can provide excellent care to our patients."

DR. GRANT OLSON (on camera):
"Implant dentistry is about giving people back all of life's moments. If you have to think about your teeth, those moments are not there. I want to dedicate my life to helping my patients and a team rally around the cause of making sure everybody lives full every one of life's moments."

DR. MITCHEL S. GODAT (on camera):
"SGA supports us and manages things behind the scenes. Our partnership allows us to focus on changing lives."

DR. MATT CAVENDISH (on camera):
"When you have been in dentistry for 20 years like I have, sometimes it is not as fun as it used to be. To be able to do a complex case like this, with somebody who is also working at a very high skill level -- it is fun."

LAMONTE JENSEN (on camera):
"What I would like to do is live up to our name. I would like to be on the cutting edge of DSOs -- of dentistry, really. I would like to gather a group of very smart, very successful dentists together who like to collaborate so we can change the game a little bit."

[MUSIC: Emotional, human, warm]

NARRATOR (V.O.):
"100+ partners. 10+ states. 85% staff retention. $350 million credit facility. Three verticals. One platform.

These are not numbers on a pitch deck. They are the outputs of a model that was built to be different -- and is."
sections.bRollDirection: Direct-to-camera testimonials from named partners in their practice settings. Authentic lighting, not over-produced. Real people in real offices. Intercut with footage of those partners treating patients, working with their teams.
sections.notes: Social proof section relies on the power of real voices. These must feel authentic -- not scripted, not corporate. The strength is in the specificity of each quote.

sections.name: The Offer
sections.script:
NARRATOR (V.O.):
"Here is how it works.

SGA's partnership model is a joint-venture equity structure. You sell a portion of your practice equity and receive meaningful liquidity at closing -- 60 to 70 percent of your valuation in cash. You retain 30 to 40 percent as equity in the SGA platform.

That means your financial upside does not end at closing. As the platform grows -- through additional partnerships, organic growth, and operational improvements -- your equity appreciates. This is the second bite of the apple. And based on the Thurston Group's historical returns, that second bite can be worth more than the first.

What changes after partnership:

Billing, payroll, HR, compliance, procurement, marketing, and recruiting transition to SGA central operations within 60 to 90 days. You keep practicing. They handle the business.

What stays the same:

Your name. Your team. Your patients. Your clinical decisions. Your schedule. Your materials. Your lab. Your way of practicing. Every partner in the SGA network can confirm this.

What you gain:

Access to the Pikos Institute. The 4Front Symposium. The Individual Growth Engine. A peer community of 100+ clinicians who share cases, teach techniques, and push each other to grow. Benefits that upgrade your team: Cigna health insurance, 401(k) through Voya with employer match, PTO, CE stipends.

And the thing nobody talks about but every partner mentions: time. The 15 hours a week you spend on non-clinical work comes back. Monday mornings feel different. Sunday nights feel different. QuickBooks no longer calls you to the back office."

[B-ROLL: Clean graphics showing the JV equity structure. Before/after of owner's weekly time allocation. Team members working with modern systems (iSolved, digital workflows). Partners at 4Front Symposium.]

[MUSIC: Clear, confident, forward-moving]
sections.bRollDirection: Clean motion graphics showing the equity model. Split-screen before/after of the owner's week. Modern office technology replacing manual processes. Partners collaborating at 4Front.
sections.notes: The offer section must be clear and specific without being salesy. The tone is educational -- explaining how the model works, not pushing a close.

sections.name: CTA and Close
sections.script:
NARRATOR (V.O.):
"Here is what I want you to do next.

Do not call us. Not yet.

First, take the Partnership Readiness Scorecard. It is a 5-minute diagnostic that measures your practice across five dimensions: Practice Performance, Operational Independence, Clinical Growth, Market Positioning, and Personal Alignment.

You will receive a personalized analysis with specific recommendations based on YOUR situation -- not a generic pitch, not a brochure, not a sales deck. An honest assessment of where you stand and what your options are.

It is free. It is confidential. And it works against any DSO, not just SGA.

Second, if the scorecard resonates, book a confidential discovery call. 15 minutes. No commitment. You talk, we listen. If SGA is not the right fit, we will tell you -- and we will still give you the framework to evaluate everyone else.

Third, ask to talk to a current partner. We will give you phone numbers, not testimonials. Call them. Ask them what Monday morning looks like. Ask them if they would do it again.

The 30% who are happy made this decision with clarity. The 70% who are not made it with hope.

Choose clarity.

Take the scorecard. Start here."

[SCREEN: URL displayed prominently over clean, dark background. SGA Dental Partners logo. Tagline: 'The Partnership Platform Built by Dentists, for Dentists.']

[MUSIC: Resolves to warm, confident close]

[END]
sections.bRollDirection: Clean end screen with scorecard URL prominently displayed. SGA branding appears for the first time at the very end -- the entire VSL is education-first, brand-second. Dark background, white text, orange accent (#F36F21).
sections.notes: The CTA is a two-step: scorecard first (low commitment), then discovery call. Do NOT push directly to a sales call. The scorecard is the conversion event. A/B test: version with countdown timer vs. without.

youtubeScripts.title: Why 7 Out of 10 Dentists Regret Selling to a DSO (And How to Avoid It)
youtubeScripts.hook: Seven out of ten dentists who sell to a DSO end up frustrated or regretful. According to the Dentist Advisors podcast, one said -- and I quote -- 'I wish I would have never done this.' He had ten million dollars in cash. So what went wrong? And more importantly, how do you make sure it does not happen to you?
youtubeScripts.fullScript:
[COLD OPEN - 0:00-0:30]

Seven out of ten.

That is not marketing. That is the actual data from the Dentist Advisors podcast. Seven out of ten dentists who sell to a DSO end up frustrated, annoyed, or outright regretful.

One dentist told them: 'I wish I would have never done this.' He had $10 million in cash.

Another said: 'When you sell, you are no longer the employer -- you are the employee.'

Today I am going to walk you through exactly why this happens, the three structural mistakes that cause it, and a framework you can use to evaluate any DSO -- so you end up in the other 30%.

[INTRO - 0:30-1:00]

If you are a dental practice owner thinking about your next chapter -- whether that is 2 years from now or 10 -- this video gives you the diagnostic framework. Subscribe and hit the bell because I am going to be posting the full evaluation toolkit over the next few weeks.

[SECTION 1: WHY THE 70% ARE UNHAPPY - 1:00-4:30]

Let us start with the why. When you look at the data and the stories, three patterns emerge.

Pattern one: they chose the wrong model.

There are three partnership models in dental consolidation. The corporate acquirer -- Heartland, Aspen, DCA. They buy your practice outright. You sign an employment agreement. Your name may or may not survive. You become an employee.

The management company -- PDS is the biggest example. You keep ownership but pay 15-25% of collections as a management fee. No platform equity. No second bite.

The equity partnership -- SGA, MB2, and others. You sell a portion, retain equity in the platform, and your financial upside continues as the platform grows.

Most of the regret comes from Model 1. Dentists sign expecting partnership and discover employment. The transition from owner to employee is the single most underestimated emotional shock in dental consolidation.

Pattern two: they did not understand the equity.

The headline multiple -- 7x, 8x, 10x -- is the number that gets talked about. But the structure underneath that number determines what you actually receive.

How is EBITDA calculated? Trailing twelve months or adjusted with add-backs? What percentage is cash at close versus rolled equity? Are there earnout provisions? What are the liquidation preferences on the equity?

The same '7x deal' can mean wildly different things depending on these structural elements. And most practice owners do not know what questions to ask until it is too late.

Pattern three: they did not verify.

Every DSO in America claims clinical autonomy. It is on every website. Every pitch deck. Every first meeting.

But there is no standard definition. At one organization, clinical autonomy means you choose your lab. At another, it means you choose your lab from an approved list. At another, it means full freedom but your bonus is tied to production targets that subtly influence every treatment plan.

The dentists who ended up happy verified before they signed. They called current partners -- not the curated list, any partners. They asked: what does Monday morning look like? What surprised you? What would you do differently?

The dentists who ended up miserable took the pitch deck at face value.

[SECTION 2: THE 7-QUESTION FRAMEWORK - 4:30-10:00]

So here is the framework. Seven questions you should ask any DSO, including the ones that look great on paper.

Question 1: Who makes clinical decisions after the deal closes -- and is it in writing?

If the clinical independence clause is not in the contract, it does not exist. Verbal promises during courtship are not enforceable. Ask for the language. Ask what happens if it is violated.

Question 2: What does my practice name look like in Year 3?

Ask them to show you three practices that joined five years ago. What are their names today? If they cannot show you preserved brands -- that tells you everything.

Question 3: What is the PE sponsor's track record?

This is the most important question and the one most practice owners skip. How many times has this PE firm built a dental platform? How long have they been at it? What happened to the partners in their previous platforms? Can you talk to one of them?

If they have done this once, they are experimenting with your career. If they have done it eight times over 38 years, that is a fundamentally different conversation.

Question 4: What does the equity model look like -- the term sheet version, not the pitch deck version?

Ask for the base case, the bull case, and the bear case. If they will only show you the bull case, that is your answer.

Question 5: What operational support arrives in the first 90 days?

Walk me through week by week what changes. Who handles payroll migration? Benefits enrollment? Insurance credentialing? Who is my point of contact when something breaks at 7 AM on Tuesday?

Question 6: What is the staff retention rate across the network?

Most DSOs do not track this or will not share it. If they cannot give you the number, that IS the answer. Staff retention is the most reliable proxy for whether a practice's culture was preserved.

Question 7: Can I speak directly with three or more current partners of MY choosing?

Not partners they select. Partners you select. Go to their website, pick three at random, call them. Any organization that blocks this is telling you what those partners would say.

[SECTION 3: HOW TO USE THIS - 10:00-12:00]

Print these seven questions out. Bring them to your next DSO meeting. The answers will tell you more in 30 minutes than 30 hours of reading pitch decks.

And here is the thing about this framework: the organizations that are doing it right will welcome these questions. They will give you phone numbers, not testimonials. They will show you all three equity scenarios. They will hand you a staff retention number and say: 'verify it.'

The organizations that are not doing it right will deflect, generalize, or channel you through 'approved' references.

That contrast tells you everything.

[CLOSE - 12:00-13:00]

The 30% who are happy made this decision with clarity and a framework. The 70% who are not made it with hope and a handshake.

If you want the full evaluation toolkit -- including a printable version of the 7-question framework and a financial model comparison template -- I have linked it in the description below.

Subscribe for the next video in this series where I am going to break down dental practice valuations in 2026 -- what the multiples actually mean, how EBITDA adjustments work, and how to tell if the offer you are getting is fair.

I will see you there.
youtubeScripts.thumbnailConcept: Bold text '7 OUT OF 10' in red on dark background. Shocked/concerned face of a dentist. Lower third: 'DSO Regret Rate' with downward arrow graphic.
youtubeScripts.seoTitle: Why 7 Out of 10 Dentists Regret Selling to a DSO (And How to Avoid It)
youtubeScripts.seoDescription:
Seven out of ten dentists who sell to a DSO end up frustrated or regretful. In this video, we break down the three structural mistakes that cause DSO regret, and give you a 7-question diagnostic framework you can use to evaluate any dental partnership -- so you end up in the other 30%.

Topics covered:
- The three partnership models in dental consolidation (corporate acquirer, management company, equity partnership)
- Why the headline multiple is not the whole picture
- The 7 questions every practice owner should ask before signing with any DSO
- How to verify clinical autonomy claims before it is too late
- Why the PE sponsor matters more than the deal terms

Resources mentioned:
- Partnership Readiness Scorecard: [LINK]
- 7-Question Evaluation Framework (printable): [LINK]
- Financial Model Comparison Template: [LINK]

About SGA Dental Partners:
SGA Dental Partners is a dental partnership platform backed by the Thurston Group (38 years, 8 platforms, $4B+ returned to partners). Founded by Dr. Mitch Ellingson (practicing dentist, CCO) and Lamonte Jensen (CEO). Three verticals: community dentistry, collaborative general and specialty practice, and advanced implantology anchored by the Pikos Institute.

#dentalpractice #DSO #dentistry #practicemanagement #dentalpartnership
youtubeScripts.retentionNotes: Highest drop-off risk at 4:30 (end of problem section). Counter with: 'Now here is the framework that changes everything.' Use pattern interrupt graphics at each question number. End card should appear at 12:30 to capture viewers before natural drop-off.

youtubeScripts.title: The Truth About Dental Practice Valuations in 2026 (What the Multiples Actually Mean)
youtubeScripts.hook: You have been told your practice is worth 7x EBITDA. Maybe 8x. Maybe 10x if you are in a hot market. But here is what nobody tells you: the same practice can be valued at $4 million or $7 million depending on how EBITDA is calculated, what percentage is cash, and whether there is an earnout. Today I am going to decode the math that DSOs use -- so you never sit across a table and get surprised.
youtubeScripts.fullScript:
[COLD OPEN - 0:00-0:30]

You have heard the numbers. 7x. 8x. 10x. Dental practice valuations are at or near cyclical highs in 2026, and every DSO in America is quoting you a multiple.

But here is the thing about multiples: the number on the pitch deck and the number on your bank statement can be very different things. And the gap between those two numbers is where practice owners lose hundreds of thousands of dollars.

Today I am going to walk you through exactly how dental practice valuations work, what the multiples actually mean, and three things your financial advisor should be asking that almost none of them do.

[INTRO - 0:30-1:00]

I am Lamonte Jensen, CEO of SGA Dental Partners. Before dental, I was a Marine Corps infantry officer and McKinsey consultant. Today I run a dental partnership platform backed by the Thurston Group -- 38 years, 8 platforms, $4 billion returned to partners. I am going to give you the financial education that most DSOs would prefer you do not have.

Subscribe -- because the next video in this series covers the three partnership models and which one is right for your specific situation.

[SECTION 1: EBITDA DECODED - 1:00-4:00]

Let us start with EBITDA, because that is the number that determines your valuation.

EBITDA stands for Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization. For a dental practice, it is essentially your operating profit before non-cash charges and financing costs.

For a practice doing $2.4 million in revenue with healthy margins, the EBITDA might be $600,000 to $800,000.

But here is where the games start.

Trailing twelve month EBITDA versus adjusted EBITDA. The trailing twelve month number is straightforward -- your actual earnings over the last 12 months. Adjusted EBITDA adds back owner compensation, discretionary expenses, one-time costs, and sometimes pro-forma synergies.

The same practice can have an EBITDA of $600,000 using trailing twelve months or $900,000 using adjusted methodology. A 7x deal on $600K is $4.2 million. A 7x deal on $900K is $6.3 million.

That is a $2.1 million difference on the same practice, at the same multiple.

So when a DSO quotes you a multiple, the first question is not what is the multiple. The first question is: what EBITDA methodology are you using, and can I see the calculation?

[SECTION 2: CASH VERSUS EQUITY - 4:00-7:00]

The second thing most practice owners miss is the split between cash at close and retained equity.

A 7x deal does not mean you receive 7x in cash. The structure matters enormously.

In a corporate acquirer model -- Heartland, DCA, Aspen -- you typically receive all cash at close. You sign an employment agreement. Your financial upside ends. The multiple IS your payout.

In an equity partnership model -- SGA, MB2, and others -- the structure is different. You receive 60-70% in cash at close and retain 30-40% as equity in the combined platform.

So on a $5 million deal at 65% cash, your immediate liquidity is $3.25 million -- not $5 million. The remaining $1.75 million is equity in the platform.

Now, here is why this matters: the equity is not worth zero. It is ownership in a growing entity. If the platform appreciates -- through additional partnerships, organic growth, and operational improvements -- your equity appreciates with it.

This is called the second bite of the apple. And industry analysts have written about this directly: in many cases, the retained minority equity position will be far more valuable in the future than if the doctor had kept 100% of the practice.

But -- and this is the critical caveat -- the second bite only works if the PE sponsor has a track record of creating that appreciation. A PE firm with a 3-year hold and 2x returns is running a different playbook than one with a 38-year history and $4 billion returned.

The question your financial advisor should ask: what is the PE sponsor's historical return to limited partners across prior healthcare platforms, and what was the median hold period?

[SECTION 3: THE EARNOUT TRAP - 7:00-9:00]

The third thing to watch for: earnouts.

Some deals include a 15-20% earnout component tied to revenue retention or EBITDA maintenance over 2-3 years.

On paper, this looks reasonable. Hit your numbers, get paid. But in practice, earnouts create perverse dynamics.

If your hygienist leaves -- something completely outside your control -- your revenue dips and you may not hit the earnout threshold. If a pandemic disrupts your schedule for six months -- again, outside your control -- the earnout may not be met.

And here is the structural problem: the DSO now has a financial incentive for you NOT to hit your earnout, because that is money they do not have to pay. I am not saying any DSO deliberately sabotages earnouts. I am saying the incentive structure is misaligned.

My advice: if a deal includes an earnout, ask three questions. What are the specific metrics? What happens if I miss them due to factors outside my control? And can I see the earnout performance data for your last 10 partnerships?

[SECTION 4: WHAT YOUR ADVISOR SHOULD ASK - 9:00-10:30]

Let me give you three questions your financial advisor should be asking but almost certainly is not.

One: what is the PE sponsor's historical return to limited partners across prior healthcare platforms, and what was the median hold period?

Two: can I see a pro-forma of what my equity looks like under the base case, the bull case, and the bear case? If they will only show you the bull case, walk away.

Three: what are the liquidation preferences on the equity I am retaining? This is a technical question, but it determines whether your equity is real or paper. Liquidation preferences can mean that the PE sponsor gets paid first in any exit event, and your equity only has value after their preferred return is met.

[CLOSE - 10:30-11:00]

Dental practice valuations in 2026 are strong. But a strong multiple means nothing if you do not understand the structure underneath it.

Take these questions to your financial advisor. Bring them to your next DSO meeting. The organizations that answer them transparently are the ones worth your time.

Link to the printable valuation checklist is in the description. Subscribe for the next video where I break down the three partnership models and which one matches your specific situation.

I will see you there.
youtubeScripts.thumbnailConcept: Calculator graphic with dental practice on it. Bold text: 'YOUR PRACTICE VALUE IN 2026' with question mark. Professional headshot of Jensen in corner.
youtubeScripts.seoTitle: The Truth About Dental Practice Valuations in 2026 | EBITDA Multiples Explained
youtubeScripts.seoDescription:
Dental practice valuations in 2026 are at cyclical highs -- but the headline multiple and your actual payout can be very different numbers. In this video, Lamonte Jensen (CEO, SGA Dental Partners; ex-McKinsey, ex-Marine) breaks down how EBITDA is calculated, why cash-at-close versus equity structure matters, and the earnout traps that cost practice owners hundreds of thousands of dollars.

You will learn:
- How trailing twelve month vs. adjusted EBITDA changes your valuation by millions
- Why a 7x multiple can mean $4.2M or $6.3M for the same practice
- The second bite of the apple -- how equity partnerships create long-term value
- Three questions your financial advisor should be asking about PE sponsors
- How to evaluate earnout provisions and protect yourself

#dentalpracticevaluation #DSO #dentistry #EBITDA #practicemanagement
youtubeScripts.retentionNotes: Financial content risks losing viewers who are not numbers-oriented. Use clear graphics at every calculation. Show the math visually, not just verbally. Pattern interrupt at the 7:00 mark with the earnout trap section -- this is where engagement typically recovers because earnouts trigger emotional response.

youtubeScripts.title: DSO vs Private Practice vs Dental Partnership: Which Model Is Right for You?
youtubeScripts.hook: There are three models for running a dental practice in 2026: go it alone, sell to a corporate DSO, or join an equity partnership. Every one of them has trade-offs that nobody talks about honestly. Today I am going to lay out the real structural differences -- the ones that determine what your life looks like 3 years from now -- so you can make this decision with clarity, not hope.
youtubeScripts.fullScript:
[COLD OPEN - 0:00-0:30]

Every dentist who owns a practice eventually faces this question: what is the next chapter?

Keep going alone? Sell to a corporate DSO? Join an equity partnership?

The internet is full of opinions. Forum posts. Podcast episodes. LinkedIn hot takes. But most of the content is either pro-DSO propaganda or anti-DSO fear-mongering. Nobody lays out the structural differences honestly.

Today I am going to fix that. Three models. Real trade-offs. No spin.

[INTRO - 0:30-1:00]

I am Dr. Mitch Ellingson. I have been a practicing dentist for over 20 years. I have owned multiple practices in Phoenix. I co-founded Gen4 Dental Partners, now part of SGA Dental Partners. I have personally lived the transition from private practice to partnership -- and I will tell you right now, it is harder than anyone anticipates.

Subscribe because this series gives you the framework to make this decision clearly.

[MODEL 1: PRIVATE PRACTICE - 1:00-5:00]

Let us start with private practice, because that is what you know.

The upside is obvious: total control. You choose your materials, your lab, your schedule, your team, your treatment plans. Nobody tells you what to do. The practice is yours.

But let us be honest about the downside, because the profession does not talk about this enough.

You are one person doing seven jobs. Dentist, CEO, HR director, marketing manager, IT department, compliance officer, and recruiter. The ADA estimates the average practice owner spends 10-15 hours per week on non-clinical tasks. Some owners I talk to say it is closer to 20.

The staffing crisis is real. 76% of practices report hygienist recruiting as extremely challenging. When your hygienist leaves, YOU are the one posting on DentalPost, interviewing, negotiating, onboarding. The search can take 6-18 months.

The financial ceiling is also real. Solo practitioners are limited by their production capacity, their overhead structure, and their ability to invest in technology and marketing. Revenue growth of 3-5% is typical. Breaking past that requires capital, infrastructure, and bandwidth that most solo owners simply do not have.

And the isolation. This is the one nobody talks about. You practice alone. You make decisions alone. You carry the stress alone. There is no peer community, no mentor, no one to call when a case goes sideways at 4 PM on a Friday.

Dr. Matt Cavendish described it perfectly: 'When you have been in dentistry for 20 years, sometimes it is not as fun as it used to be.' That is not burnout from dentistry. That is burnout from isolation.

Private practice works. It has worked for decades. But the question is not whether it works -- it is whether it is sustainable for YOU, for the next 10-15 years, given what the market looks like today.

[MODEL 2: CORPORATE DSO - 5:00-9:00]

Model 2 is the corporate acquirer. Heartland, Aspen, DCA, and others.

The structure: they buy your practice outright. You receive cash at closing. You sign an employment agreement and stay on for 3-5 years. Your name may or may not survive. Your clinical decisions become increasingly influenced by corporate protocols.

The upside: the check. Corporate acquirers often offer the highest day-one multiples because they are buying 100% of your practice. If your goal is maximum cash now and you do not care about what happens to the practice after you leave, this model can deliver.

But here is the downside, and it is significant.

Your financial upside ends at closing. There is no second act. No platform equity. No participation in the growth of the organization. You become a salaried employee -- sometimes with a production bonus, sometimes without.

Clinical autonomy erodes. Not always immediately. But over time, corporate protocols expand. Regional managers -- who are often not dentists -- begin influencing decisions. Glassdoor reviews of the largest DSOs describe 'crown days' and production pressure that subtly but powerfully shapes treatment planning.

Your name disappears. Aspen rebrands every practice. Heartland rebrands most. DCA rebrands many. The community reputation you built over 20 years becomes their corporate brand.

And staff turnover. When a corporate acquirer changes the culture -- even subtly -- staff notices. The office manager who has been with you for 15 years feels the shift. The hygienist who chose your practice because of your culture starts looking at other options.

This is where the 7-out-of-10 statistic comes from. Most of the regret in dental consolidation comes from dentists who chose Model 2 expecting partnership and discovered employment.

[MODEL 3: EQUITY PARTNERSHIP - 9:00-13:00]

Model 3 is the equity partnership. This is the model SGA uses, and it is the model I chose for my own career.

The structure: you sell a portion of your practice equity -- typically 55-70% -- and receive meaningful liquidity at closing. But you retain 30-45% as equity in the combined platform. Your financial upside continues as the platform grows.

Clinical autonomy is not just a talking point -- it is contractual. At SGA, the Clinical Independence Clause specifies that treatment plans, materials, lab selection, scheduling, and clinical protocols remain with the partner dentist. The CCO is a practicing dentist -- that would be me -- not a corporate executive.

Your name stays. Dental Partners of Southwest Georgia. Periodontal Associates of Memphis. Innovative Dental. Five years later. Ten years later.

The education platform matters. This is the differentiator that most practice owners do not think to evaluate. At SGA, partners have access to the Pikos Institute, the 4Front Symposium, and the Individual Growth Engine. This is not a CE stipend. This is a clinical development ecosystem that transforms careers.

But I want to be honest about the trade-offs.

You are giving up a portion of control. Yes, clinical decisions stay with you. But operational decisions -- billing, payroll, HR, marketing -- transition to the platform. For most owners, this is a relief. But for some, it feels like loss.

The day-one check is smaller. Because you are retaining equity, the cash at closing is 60-70% of your valuation, not 100%. The bet is that the equity appreciation -- the second bite -- will more than compensate. And based on the Thurston Group's track record ($4B+ returned across 8 platforms), that bet has been historically sound. But it is not guaranteed.

Not all equity partnerships are equal. There are over 1,000 organizations calling themselves IDSOs. Fewer than 100 are considered qualified by industry experts. The PE sponsor, the deal structure, the clinical culture, the education platform -- all of these vary enormously.

[COMPARISON AND CLOSE - 13:00-16:00]

Let me put this side by side.

If your priority is maximum cash now and you are 2-3 years from full retirement, Model 2 -- the corporate acquirer -- may be the right financial decision. Just go in with clear eyes about what the post-deal experience looks like.

If your priority is total independence and you have the team, the systems, and the energy to keep running the business for another 10-15 years, Model 1 -- private practice -- is a viable path. But honestly assess whether those conditions are sustainable.

If your priority is clinical growth, operational relief, financial diversification, and a peer community -- while maintaining your name, your team, and your clinical autonomy -- Model 3 is what I would evaluate first. And I say that as someone who made exactly that choice.

Here is the framework I use with every practice owner who asks me this question: forget the models for a minute. Answer three questions.

What do you want Monday morning to look like in 3 years?
What are you willing to give up to get there?
What can you verify about the organization you are considering?

The answers to those three questions will lead you to the right model.

If you want to evaluate where your practice stands right now, take the Partnership Readiness Scorecard -- link in the description. 5 minutes, confidential, and it works against any model.

Subscribe for the next video where I take you inside what actually happens after you join a DSO -- the 90-day transition, the first-year reality, and what partners wish they had known.

See you there.

youtubeScripts.seoDescription:
Should you stay in private practice, sell to a corporate DSO, or join an equity partnership? Dr. Mitch Ellingson -- practicing dentist, 20+ years experience, CCO of SGA Dental Partners -- walks through all three models with honest trade-offs.

This video covers:
- Private practice: the real cost of isolation and administrative burden
- Corporate DSOs (Heartland, Aspen, DCA): what the 7-out-of-10 regret rate actually means
- Equity partnerships: how JV models work and who they are right for
- The 3 questions that will lead you to the right model
- Why the PE sponsor matters more than the multiple

#DSO #privatepractice #dentistry #dentalpartnership #practicemanagement
youtubeScripts.retentionNotes: Comparison content retains well because viewers want to hear about their specific model. Use clear section titles and chapter markers. The highest-value moment is the side-by-side comparison at 13:00 -- tease this in the intro. Drop-off risk at 9:00 (transition between DSO and equity sections); counter with 'Now let me tell you about the model I chose for my own career.'

youtubeScripts.title: What Really Happens After You Join a DSO (The First Year, Unfiltered)
youtubeScripts.hook: Every DSO shows you the pitch deck. Nobody shows you what the first Monday morning looks like after you sign. Today I am going to walk you through exactly what happens in the first 90 days, the first 6 months, and the first year of a dental partnership -- the good, the hard, and the things nobody prepares you for.
youtubeScripts.fullScript:
[COLD OPEN - 0:00-0:30]

Every DSO will show you the transaction. The multiple. The equity structure. The marketing brochure with stock photos of smiling dentists.

Nobody shows you what Tuesday at 7 AM looks like three weeks after you sign.

Today I am pulling back the curtain. The first 90 days. The first six months. The first year. What goes well, what is hard, and the things that nobody -- including us -- prepared our partners for.

[SECTION 1: THE FIRST 90 DAYS - 1:00-5:00]

The first 90 days are a transition. And transition is a polite word for controlled chaos.

Here is what actually happens at SGA in the first month.

Week 1: You sign the papers. There is a moment of euphoria mixed with terror. This is normal. Every partner we have ever had describes this exact feeling. One partner called it 'the best and scariest decision I have ever made, simultaneously.'

A dedicated transition team is assigned to your practice. Not a department -- names. Specific people who own specific tasks. Payroll migration to iSolved. Benefits enrollment. Insurance credentialing review. Supply chain assessment.

Week 2-4: The operational transition begins. This is where most of the friction lives. Your office manager, who has been running payroll in QuickBooks for 12 years, is now learning a new system. Your billing person is adjusting to centralized processes. Your front desk is getting access to new scheduling tools.

Spencer Pierce, our VP of People and Culture, describes what he sees: 'After onboarding is completed, there is an abundance of relief. It is like, okay, I understand what this means for me now.'

But that relief does not come on Day 1. It comes after the friction of change subsides. Usually around Week 4-6.

Month 2-3: The operational burden starts lifting. The things that used to consume your evenings -- payroll, compliance, vendor negotiations, recruiting -- are being handled. You start coming home earlier. Your office manager stops wearing 15 hats and starts wearing two.

And here is the thing nobody tells you: the hardest part of the first 90 days is not the systems or the processes. It is the identity shift. You went from being the person who controlled everything to being the person who controls the clinical work. That is what you wanted. But it still feels strange.

[SECTION 2: MONTHS 4-6 -- THE CLINICAL AWAKENING - 5:00-9:00]

Months 4-6 are where the magic happens. And I use that word deliberately, because it surprises almost every partner.

The operational burden is gone. The bandwidth that was consumed by admin is now available. And what happens next is what separates SGA from every other DSO I have seen.

The Individual Growth Engine kicks in. You are paired with a clinical mentor based on your goals, not your geography. If you want to learn implants, you are paired with someone who has done 5,000 of them. If you want to expand into full-arch, you get access to the Pikos Institute.

Dr. Matt Cavendish is the proof case. 20 years in practice. Good numbers. Clinically competent but stagnating. After joining, he was mentored, observed his first all-on-four, and within months was performing complex cases he had never attempted.

His revenue? Up 16.5% trailing twelve months. Staff retention? 100%. EBITDA beating plan by 2.3 points.

But here is what Matt talks about: 'To be able to do a complex case like this, with somebody who is also working at a very high skill level -- it is fun.'

Fun. That word. After 20 years.

This is not an outlier. This is the pattern. When you remove the administrative burden and provide clinical community and mentorship, dentists rediscover why they went into dentistry.

[SECTION 3: THE FIRST YEAR REALITY - 9:00-12:30]

By the end of Year 1, here is what most SGA partners report.

The operational transition is complete. Systems are running. The team has adapted. The initial friction is a memory.

Clinical growth is underway. New procedures, new confidence, new peer relationships. The 4Front Symposium -- our annual gathering -- is usually the moment partners describe as transformative. 100+ doctors in a room, sharing cases, teaching techniques, challenging each other.

One partner described it: 'Senior clinicians brainstorming together naturally elevated the ideas together. That is where the goose bumps come from.'

Financial clarity has arrived. The equity model makes sense now because you can see the platform growing. New partnerships joining, revenue increasing, the value of your retained equity appreciating.

And the personal impact. This is what I hear most often, and it is the thing you cannot put on a spreadsheet. Partners tell me they are present at dinner again. They are coaching their kid's team again. They are sleeping through the night instead of waking up at 3 AM worrying about the schedule.

Dr. Brian Harris put it simply: 'This organization is fully invested in us -- in us loving what we do and being successful in what we do so we can provide excellent care to our patients.'

That is Year 1.

[SECTION 4: WHAT NOBODY PREPARES YOU FOR - 12:30-13:30]

Let me be honest about the things that are hard, because I promised you unfiltered.

The identity shift is real. Going from owner-of-everything to clinician-in-a-network takes emotional adjustment. Some partners sail through it. Others struggle for months.

The pace of change can feel overwhelming. New systems, new contacts, new processes -- all at once. Even when the changes are positive, change is change. And it takes energy.

Not every partner experience is identical. Some practices transition seamlessly. Others have hiccups -- a billing migration that takes longer than expected, a team member who resists the new systems, a supply chain adjustment that creates temporary friction. These are real, and pretending they do not happen would be dishonest.

But here is what I would tell any practice owner considering this: the friction is temporary. The relief is permanent. And the clinical growth -- the mentorship, the community, the education platform -- is the thing that makes it worth it.

[CLOSE - 13:30-14:00]

If you want to hear this directly from a current partner -- not from me, not from a marketing video -- I will connect you. We give out phone numbers, not testimonials.

Take the Partnership Readiness Scorecard in the description. And subscribe for the next video, where I take you inside SGA Dental Partners -- the three verticals, the Pikos Institute, the 4Front Symposium, and what makes this platform structurally different from every other DSO in the market.

See you there.

youtubeScripts.seoDescription:
What does the first year inside a dental partnership actually look like? Dr. Mitch Ellingson -- practicing dentist and CCO of SGA Dental Partners -- walks through the real timeline: the first 90 days of operational transition, the clinical awakening in months 4-6, and the Year 1 reality that nobody shows you on a pitch deck.

Includes:
- What the first Monday morning looks like after signing
- The identity shift from owner to clinician-in-a-network
- How Dr. Cavendish went from stagnation to 16.5% revenue growth
- The things that are genuinely hard (and how to prepare)
- Why 'fun' is the word partners use most often after Year 1

#DSO #dentalpartnership #dentistry #firstyear #practicemanagement
youtubeScripts.retentionNotes: This video has strong retention potential because it satisfies curiosity about the unknown. The highest-value section is 'What Nobody Prepares You For' at 12:30 -- tease this in the intro. Use timeline graphics (Day 1, Week 2, Month 3, etc.) to give visual structure. The Matt Cavendish story at 7:00 is the emotional peak -- match with his actual photo/footage.

youtubeScripts.title: Inside SGA Dental Partners: The Platform Built by Dentists, for Dentists
youtubeScripts.hook: Most DSOs will show you a pitch deck. Today I am going to show you the inside of a dental partnership platform -- the three verticals, the education ecosystem, the PE sponsor, and the partner stories -- so you can decide for yourself whether this is the model that matches what you are looking for.
youtubeScripts.fullScript:
[COLD OPEN - 0:00-0:30]

If you have watched the previous videos in this series, you know the framework. You know the three partnership models. You know the seven questions. You know how valuations work.

Today I am going to apply all of that -- to us. SGA Dental Partners. Under the same scrutiny I told you to apply to everyone else.

Because if the framework works, it should work on us too.

[SECTION 1: WHO WE ARE - 0:30-3:00]

SGA Dental Partners is a dental partnership platform built on three verticals.

Vertical one: Community Dentistry. This is the original SGA network -- hometown practices across the Southeast. Georgia, Mississippi, Alabama, Louisiana, Tennessee. These are community institutions, some operating for decades, serving the families who built them. Dental Partners of Southwest Georgia. Garner Dental Group, across 23 offices.

Vertical two: Collaborative General and Specialty Practice. This is Gen4 -- the national footprint. Arizona, California, Nevada, Minnesota, Michigan, Texas, and beyond. Built around a peer collaboration culture where dentists teach dentists.

Vertical three: Advanced Implantology. This is MODIS -- anchored by Dr. Michael Pikos and the Pikos Institute. If you are in the implant space, you know who Dr. Pikos is. The Pikos Institute is the gold standard in implantology education worldwide. Clinical innovators like Dr. Grant Olson -- 138,000 YouTube subscribers, one of 300 CEREC Mentors worldwide, patented guided implant technology -- are the clinicians who define this vertical.

No other DSO in America has this three-vertical architecture. A general dentist, a periodontist, and a pediatric practice all have a specifically designed home inside SGA.

[SECTION 2: THE EDUCATION PLATFORM - 3:00-6:00]

This is the differentiator. And I do not say that lightly.

The Pikos Institute. Dr. Michael Pikos is a pioneer in dental implantology. The Pikos Institute is not a CE catalog -- it is a hands-on, immersive clinical training center that has trained thousands of dentists worldwide. SGA partners have access to this.

The 4Front Symposium. This is our annual gathering. Think of it as the opposite of a corporate dental conference. No vendor booths. No corporate presentations. 100+ partner clinicians in a room sharing cases, teaching techniques, pushing each other. One partner said: 'Senior clinicians brainstorming together naturally elevated the ideas together. That is where the goose bumps come from.'

The Individual Growth Engine. This is 1-on-1 mentorship. You are paired with an experienced clinician based on your clinical goals. Dr. Cavendish was paired with a mentor, observed his first all-on-four, and within months was performing complex cases he had never attempted. Revenue up 16.5%. Staff retention 100%.

Ask any DSO you are evaluating: what is your education platform? If their answer is 'we offer a CE stipend,' that is a benefit. It is not a platform.

[SECTION 3: THE PE SPONSOR - 6:00-8:00]

The Thurston Group.

Founded in 1988 by Patrick J. Haynes III. 38 years in business. Eight dental and healthcare platforms built. Smile Doctors. US Endodontics Partners. US Oral Surgery Management. ARC Health. US Orthopaedic Partners.

Over $4 billion returned to partners across the firm's history. That is not a projection. It is historical.

SGA has a $350 million credit facility. Gen4 has a $315 million credit facility. This is growth capital for the platform, not debt loaded onto your practice.

I told you in the previous videos to ask: who is behind the money, and what is their track record? At SGA, the answer is transparent, verifiable, and available for direct conversation. The Thurston principals -- Patrick Haynes III and Daniel Davis -- take calls from prospective partners.

And we will connect you with partners from PRIOR Thurston platforms who have been through a full investment cycle, including an exit. No other DSO can offer that.

[SECTION 4: THE PARTNER STORIES - 8:00-10:00]

Dr. Mitchel S. Godat, Periodontal Associates of Memphis. Still his name. Still his team. Still his way of practicing. His words: 'SGA supports us and manages things behind the scenes. Our partnership allows us to focus on changing lives.'

Dr. Matt Cavendish, Phoenix. 20 years in practice. Revenue up 16.5%. Staff retention 100%. 'It is fun again.'

Dr. Grant Olson, Innovative Dental, Springfield, Missouri. 138,000 YouTube subscribers. 23 million views. 3,390+ five-star reviews. One of the most visible dentists in America -- and a MODIS partner.

Dr. Brian Harris: 'This organization is fully invested in us -- in us loving what we do and being successful in what we do so we can provide excellent care to our patients.'

These are not testimonials I selected. These are phone numbers I will give you. Call any of them. Ask what Monday morning looks like.

[SECTION 5: THE DEAL STRUCTURE - 10:00-10:30]

Joint-venture equity partnership. 60-70% cash at closing. 30-40% retained as equity in the SGA platform. Clinical independence clause in the contract. 85% staff retention rate published -- no competitor publishes theirs. Benefits upgrade: Cigna, Mutual of Omaha, 401(k) through Voya, PTO.

The first 90 days: billing, payroll, HR, compliance, procurement, marketing, and recruiting transition to central operations. You keep practicing.

[CLOSE - 10:30-11:00]

I told you in the first video to apply the seven-question framework to everyone. Apply it to us.

Who makes clinical decisions? You do. In writing.
What does my name look like in Year 3? The same.
What is the PE track record? 38 years. 8 platforms. $4B+.
What does the equity look like? We show you all three scenarios.
What happens in the first 90 days? We have a playbook.
Staff retention? 85%. Published.
Can I talk to partners? All of them.

Take the Partnership Readiness Scorecard -- link in the description. If your score suggests we should talk, book a confidential discovery call.

I will not pitch you. I will listen. And if SGA is not the right fit, I will tell you -- and I will still give you the framework to evaluate everyone else.

That is the promise. Take the scorecard.
